summary: Widely cited claims from Apple researcher Bruce Tognazzini that mice are faster than keyboards for all tasks are based on unreproducible studies and one bogus microbenchmark. When the author actually tests various tasks (search-and-replace, text selection, scrolling), results are mixed and predictable: keyboards excel at some tasks, mice at others. The literature on keyboard vs. mouse speed is plagued by issues including outdated studies from the 1980s, artificial microbenchmarks, vague descriptions, and tests on unskilled users that don't generalize to experienced programmers.
key_points: AskTog claims based on $50M Apple R&D are unreproducible - no experimental details or data are provided, just blanket assertions
The one cited experiment (replacing 'e' with '|' using cursor keys vs mouse) is bizarrely artificial and disallows basic editor features like search-and-replace
Author's own tests show mixed results: keyboard is 2x faster for search-and-replace (1.26s vs 5.39s), mouse is 2.5x faster for selecting visible text blocks (0.7s vs 1.8s), scrolling is roughly equal

Published literature is largely irrelevant due to age (1980s studies on inferior hardware), narrow microbenchmarks, vague descriptions, or testing unskilled users who take 2 seconds to move a mouse across screen
Programmers can accurately predict which input method will be faster for specific tasks, contrary to Tog's claim that users are deluded about keyboard speed
money_quotes: I'm deeply skeptical of broad claims that come from microbenchmarks unless the author has a demonstrated, deep, understanding of benchmarking their particular domain, and even then I'll ask why they believe their result generalizes.
The e -> | replacement task described is not only a microbenchmark, it's a bizarrely artificial microbenchmark.

There's nothing wrong with studying populations who aren't relatively expert at using computer input devices, but there is something wrong with generalizing those results to people who are relatively expert.

It's entirely possible that the mysterious studies Tog's org spent $50M on prove that the mouse is faster than the keyboard for all tasks other than raw text input, but there doesn't appear to be enough information to tell what the actual studies were.
This result is so boring that it would barely be worth mentioning if not for the large groups of people who believe that either the keyboard is always faster than the mouse or vice versa.
key_insight: The influential Apple studies claiming mice are universally faster than keyboards are based on unreproducible research and artificial microbenchmarks that ignore how skilled users actually work, while empirical testing shows the obvious truth that each input method excels at different tasks.
